meta.name: Injera (Fermented Teff Flatbread)
meta.subtitle: Ethiopian sourdough-style fermented flatbread with a spongy texture and signature “eyes.”

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: This is a production-ready baseline injera recipe designed for SSA catalogs (fermentation-aware).
Includes both traditional teff-only and common teff+all-purpose blend variation paths.
Fermentation time varies by temperature; this recipe includes checks and decision points.

summary: Injera is a fermented teff flatbread with a tangy flavor and spongy texture used as both plate and utensil in Ethiopian meals.

yield.notes: Makes ~8–12 injera (28–32 cm) depending on pan size and batter thickness.

required.label: Large nonstick skillet or mitad + lid
Injera spreader (optional) or quick swirl technique

optional.label: Mitad (Ethiopian injera griddle)
optional.notes: Best results; a wide nonstick pan also works.

notes: 100% teff injera is gluten-free if teff is not cross-contaminated.
The optional blend version includes wheat flour and is not gluten-free.

ingredients.label: Teff flour
notes: Traditional injera uses teff flour.
If using a teff blend, see variations for adjusted flour ratios.

ingredients.label: Water (room temperature, for batter)
notes: Start with 850–900 ml; adjust consistency after fermentation and absit.
Chlorinated water can slow fermentation; filtered water is recommended.

ingredients.label: Ersho (traditional starter) or active sourdough starter
notes: If you have ersho from a prior injera batch, use it for the most traditional result.
If no ersho, use 60–100 g active sourdough starter.
If neither is available, you can ferment naturally (wild), but time is less predictable.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: Add after fermentation (salt can slow fermentation if added early).

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (for pan coating, optional)
ingredients.notes: Many nonstick setups need little to none; use sparingly.

ingredients.label: Absit portion (reserved batter + water; see steps)
notes: Absit is a cooked paste made from a portion of batter and water to improve texture and ‘eyes.’
You will make this from your own batter (no separate ingredient purchase).
Fermentation target: pleasantly sour aroma, slight tang taste, and visible bubbling.
A thin watery layer (“hooch”) may form on top—this is normal. Pour it off or stir in depending on taste preference.
Injera cooks with steam under a lid—no flipping. The top should set and become matte with visible ‘eyes.’
Pan temperature is critical: too hot burns before eyes form; too cool yields dense injera.

changes.flour: Use 300 g teff flour + 200 g all-purpose flour (instead of 500 g teff).
changes.water: Start with 850 ml water (wheat absorbs differently).
changes.notes: This version is easier to get consistent ‘eyes’ with standard nonstick pans but is not gluten-free.

changes.starter: Omit starter; ferment batter 3–6 days depending on temperature.
changes.notes: Expect higher variability. Use sensory checks (smell/taste/bubbles) to decide readiness.
Check at 24–36 hours; avoid overheating which can produce harsh sourness.
Pour off hooch before mixing; reduce fermentation time once tang is present.

notes: Fermentation should smell pleasantly sour, not putrid or moldy.
Discard if you see fuzzy mold, pink/orange growth, or smell strongly rotten.
Keep container loosely covered to prevent contaminants while allowing gas release.
Cool fully before storing to prevent condensation and sticking.
Freeze with parchment between layers; thaw covered to retain softness.

fridge.notes: Store stacked with parchment between pieces in an airtight bag or container.

freezer.notes: Freeze with parchment separators. Thaw covered at room temp, then briefly warm to soften.

makeAhead: Batter can be fermented ahead; after absit step, cook within 24 hours for best results.
Cooked injera stores well and supports batch meal planning for Ethiopian platters.

steps.title: Mix the batter
instructions: In a large bowl, whisk teff flour with water until smooth (no dry pockets).
If using starter (ersho or sourdough), whisk it in until evenly dispersed.
The batter should be pourable like a thin pancake batter; adjust with small amounts of water if needed.

steps.title: Ferment
instructions: Cover the bowl loosely (lid ajar or cloth) to allow gases to escape.
Ferment at room temperature 2–3 days (longer if cooler; shorter if warm).
Check daily: look for bubbles and smell for a clean sour aroma.
checks: Smell is pleasantly sour (not rotten or moldy).

steps.title: Decide about the top liquid (hooch)
instructions: If a watery layer forms on top, decide based on flavor:
• For a sharper tang: stir it back in.
• For a milder flavor: carefully pour it off, then stir the batter.
Remove any dark crusty bits on the surface if present.
checks: Batter is stirred and smells cleanly sour.

steps.title: Make absit (gelatinized binder paste)
instructions: In a small saucepan, whisk together 120 ml batter with 240 ml water until smooth.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, until it thickens to a glossy porridge/paste (2–5 minutes).
Remove from heat and cool 8–10 minutes until warm (not hot).
checks: Absit is smooth and glossy (no raw flour taste).
Absit is warm, not hot, before mixing back in.

steps.title: Finish batter (absit + salt + consistency)
instructions: Whisk the warm absit back into the fermented batter until fully incorporated.
Add salt and whisk.
Rest the batter 20–30 minutes to stabilize.
Adjust consistency: aim for a thin crepe batter that pours easily and spreads quickly. Add water 15–30 ml at a time if too thick.
checks: Batter is pourable and slightly thicker than water (thin crepe batter).
Taste is pleasantly tangy and lightly salted.

steps.title: Preheat pan and test one injera
instructions: Heat a large nonstick skillet (or mitad) over medium heat until evenly hot.
Lightly oil the surface only if needed.
Ladle batter into the pan (about 120–180 ml depending on pan size).
Immediately swirl the pan to spread batter thinly and evenly.
Cover with a lid and cook until the top is set and matte with ‘eyes’ (1–3 minutes). Do not flip.
checks: Top is set and matte; many small holes (‘eyes’) are visible.

steps.title: Cook the batch
instructions: Continue cooking injera one at a time: ladle, swirl, cover, and steam-cook until set.
Adjust heat as needed: if bottom browns too fast, lower heat; if no eyes form, slightly increase heat or thin batter.
Remove each injera to a clean towel or tray; keep covered to stay soft.
checks: Injera are soft and flexible with visible eyes.
No burning; texture is spongy, not gummy.

steps.title: Cool and store
instructions: Cool injera briefly before stacking to avoid excess condensation.
Stack with parchment if needed to prevent sticking.
Store covered at room temperature for same-day use or refrigerate/freeze as noted.
checks: Injera are cool enough to stack and store without sticking.

change.notes: Lower heat and allow pan to re-equilibrate 2–3 minutes. Consider thinning batter slightly and cooking covered longer.
Batter may be too thick or pan too cool. Thin batter 30–60 ml water, and/or increase heat slightly. Ensure lid traps steam.
Shorten fermentation next time; pour off hooch; ferment cooler; increase resting flour blend variation if acceptable.
Use 100% teff flour and verify cross-contamination risk. Do not use the wheat blend variation.
